claims.claim_text: The October 2023 San Francisco Cruise incident involved a pedestrian being dragged about 20 feet after the AV attempted a pullover maneuver.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.rationale: The incident is real and specifically documented, but the primary source is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The California DMV suspension forced Cruise to ground a nationwide fleet of 400 autonomous vehicles.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: The California DMV immediately suspended Cruise's deployment permits, forcing the company to ground its entire nationwide fleet of 400 autonomous vehicles.
claims.suggested_rewrite: California regulators suspended Cruise's deployment permits, forcing Cruise into a broader operational pause while it addressed safety and compliance issues.
claims.rationale: The exact fleet size and nationwide scope are bundled into a precise consequence chain without an allowlisted source directly backing the full sentence.

claims.claim_text: Within weeks of the incident, Cruise's CEO resigned, 24% of staff were laid off, and GM cut Cruise's valuation by more than half.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: Within weeks, the CEO resigned, the company laid off 24 percent of its workforce, and General Motors slashed Cruise's internal valuation by more than half, erasing billions of dollars in enterprise value overnight.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Within weeks, Cruise faced leadership turnover, layoffs, and a major business reset with significant financial consequences.
claims.rationale: The sentence stacks multiple exact business outcomes and magnitudes that would require several non-allowlisted sources.

claims.claim_text: The ViT-G/14 row presents an exact parameter count and accuracy figure.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 1, model table under ViT
claims.suggested_rewrite: | ViT-G/14 | 14 | 40 | 1408 | Billion-scale variant | Cite a specific source for the exact benchmark figure |
claims.rationale: I did not verify this exact row from an allowlisted primary source gathered in this run, so the precise benchmark should not stand uncited.

claims.claim_text: CLIP was trained on 400 million image-text pairs collected from the internet.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2 / Contrastive Learning: The Key Innovation
claims.rationale: The CLIP paper abstract states the 400 million pair dataset directly.

claims.claim_text: OpenCLIP is an open-source CLIP reproduction built on public data and code.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2 / CLIP Variants and Successors, OpenCLIP row
claims.rationale: The OpenCLIP scaling-laws paper explicitly discusses the public LAION dataset and the open-source OpenCLIP repository.

claims.claim_text: SigLIP replaces CLIP's softmax-normalized contrastive loss with a pairwise sigmoid loss.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2 / CLIP Variants and Successors, SigLIP row
claims.rationale: The SigLIP paper defines the method around the sigmoid loss directly.

claims.claim_text: BLIP extends vision-language pretraining to both understanding and generation tasks, including image captioning.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2 / CLIP Variants and Successors, BLIP row
claims.rationale: The BLIP paper explicitly frames the model as unified understanding-and-generation vision-language pretraining.

claims.claim_text: BLIP-2 bridges a frozen image encoder and a frozen LLM with a Querying Transformer (Q-Former).
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2 / CLIP Variants and Successors, BLIP-2 row
claims.rationale: The BLIP-2 abstract states this architecture explicitly.

claims.claim_text: EVA-CLIP is presented as an 18B-parameter open-source CLIP model.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2 / CLIP Variants and Successors, EVA-CLIP row
claims.rationale: The EVA-CLIP paper title and abstract directly support the 18B claim.

claims.claim_text: OpenAI's multimodal model is the industry standard for visual logic, OCR, and multi-image spatial reasoning.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 3 / Major Vision-Language Models in Production / GPT-5 Vision
claims.anchor_text: OpenAI continues to iterate heavily on their proprietary multimodal architecture. It remains the industry standard for complex visual logic, robust OCR, and multi-image spatial reasoning.
claims.suggested_rewrite: OpenAI offers proprietary multimodal models for image understanding, OCR-style extraction, and multi-image analysis, but model choice should be validated against your own benchmarks.
claims.rationale: The superlative ranking is not directly backed by an allowlisted primary benchmark source.

claims.claim_text: Anthropic's Claude family excels at safety-critical analysis of charts, graphs, and technical diagrams.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 3 / Major Vision-Language Models in Production / Claude 3 Vision
claims.anchor_text: Anthropic's Claude family excels at safety-critical detailed analysis, particularly concerning data-dense charts, graphs, and highly technical diagrams.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Anthropic's Claude models support image, chart, graph, and document analysis, but task-specific accuracy should be validated before safety-critical use.
claims.rationale: The sentence makes an unbenchmarked superiority claim that official docs do not directly substantiate.

claims.claim_text: LLaVA proved open models could compete with proprietary systems and be fine-tuned on modest hardware.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 3 / Major Vision-Language Models in Production / LLaVA
claims.anchor_text: The Large Language and Vision Assistant (LLaVA) proved that open-source models could compete with proprietary giants. By combining a frozen CLIP encoder with a LLaMA model, it demonstrated that highly capable VLMs could be assembled and fine-tuned on modest hardware.
claims.suggested_rewrite: LLaVA showed that open multimodal models could be built by connecting a vision encoder to an LLM, making this architecture more accessible for research and customization.
claims.rationale: The architecture is citable, but the competitiveness and modest-hardware claims are overstated without a direct benchmark source.

claims.claim_text: OpenAI gpt-5 image input costs $0.00255 per 512x512 tile.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 6 / Pricing Comparison table, OpenAI gpt-5 row
claims.anchor_text: | OpenAI | gpt-5 | $0.00255 per 512x512 tile | Multiple tiles for larger images |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | OpenAI | gpt-5 | Check current token-based image pricing | Cost depends on image size and detail level |
claims.rationale: Exact vendor pricing is temporally unstable and the current official pricing model is token-based rather than safely captured by this fixed figure.

claims.claim_text: OpenAI gpt-5-mini image input costs $0.000638 per 512x512 tile.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 6 / Pricing Comparison table, OpenAI gpt-5-mini row
claims.anchor_text: | OpenAI | gpt-5-mini | $0.000638 per 512x512 tile | 4x cheaper, slightly lower quality |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | OpenAI | gpt-5-mini | Check current token-based image pricing | Lower-cost variants trade off capability against price |
claims.rationale: The exact tile-price figure is not stable enough to leave uncited and may no longer match current pricing.

claims.claim_text: Claude 3 Opus costs about $0.024 per 1000 input tokens and images count as about 1500 tokens.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 6 / Pricing Comparison table, Claude 3 Opus row
claims.anchor_text: | Anthropic | Claude 3 Opus | ~$0.024 per 1000 input tokens | Images count as ~1500 tokens |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Anthropic | Claude Opus-family models | Check current token pricing | Image cost depends on current token accounting and model tier |
claims.rationale: Both the price and image-token estimate are version-specific and stale-prone.

claims.claim_text: Claude 3 Sonnet costs about $0.003 per image.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 6 / Pricing Comparison table, Claude 3 Sonnet row
claims.anchor_text: | Anthropic | Claude 3 Sonnet | ~$0.003 per image | Much cheaper for most uses |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Anthropic | Claude Sonnet-family models | Check current pricing | Use the current pricing page rather than fixed per-image estimates |
claims.rationale: This is an unstable per-image estimate tied to an older model family.

claims.claim_text: Claude 3 Haiku costs about $0.0005 per image.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 6 / Pricing Comparison table, Claude 3 Haiku row
claims.anchor_text: | Anthropic | Claude 3 Haiku | ~$0.0005 per image | Best value for simple tasks |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Anthropic | Claude Haiku-family models | Check current pricing | Use the current pricing page rather than fixed per-image estimates |
claims.rationale: The specific price is not stable and the 'best value' judgment is not an objective fact.

claims.claim_text: Gemini Pro Vision has a free tier and then costs $0.0025 per image.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 6 / Pricing Comparison table, Gemini Pro Vision row
claims.anchor_text: | Google | Gemini Pro Vision | Free tier, then $0.0025/image | Generous free quota |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Google | Gemini vision-capable models | Check current token pricing and quotas | Pricing and free-tier terms change over time |
claims.rationale: This pricing framing is stale-prone and no longer safely represented as a single fixed per-image figure.

claims.claim_text: Vision-language models have limited precise spatial localization ability and are a poor fit for exact coordinates or geometry-heavy tasks.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes table, exact spatial coordinates row
claims.rationale: Anthropic's official vision limitations explicitly note weak spatial reasoning for precise localization tasks.

claims.claim_text: AlexNet won ILSVRC 2012 with a 15.3% top-5 error rate, beating the runner-up by 10.8 percentage points.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1
claims.rationale: The cited page directly states the exact top-5 error and runner-up margin.

claims.claim_text: LAION-5B was released in March 2022 with precisely 5.85 billion pairs and required over 10,000 GPU hours for filtering.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 3
claims.anchor_text: The open-source LAION-5B dataset, released to the public in March 2022, contains precisely 5.85 billion image-text pairs and required over 10,000 GPU hours strictly to compute the embedding metrics for filtering.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Public web-scale datasets such as LAION-5B contain billions of image-text pairs and require substantial compute to curate and filter.
claims.rationale: The exact release timing, pair count, and GPU-hour number need a specific primary source that was not established here.

claims.claim_text: Tesla Autopilot processes 8 cameras at 36 FPS and generates over 1.5 GB/s of raw visual data per vehicle.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 4
claims.anchor_text: Tesla's Autopilot hardware stack processes video streams from 8 simultaneous cameras at 36 frames per second, generating over 1.5 gigabytes of raw visual data per second per vehicle to feed its distributed fleet learning networks.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Production vehicle perception stacks can ingest many camera streams at high frame rates, creating substantial data pipelines for perception and training.
claims.rationale: The vendor-specific exact numbers are not safely supported here by an allowlisted primary source.

further_reading.title: An Image is Worth 16x16 Words
further_reading.why_relevant: Foundational paper for Vision Transformers and the patch-based formulation used throughout the module.

further_reading.title: Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for CLIP, contrastive image-text learning, and zero-shot transfer.

further_reading.title: BLIP-2
further_reading.why_relevant: Clear primary reference for the modern frozen-vision-encoder plus LLM bridge pattern used by many VLMs.

notes: Excluded code blocks, Mermaid diagrams, hands-on exercise steps, and knowledge-check items per schema rules. The highest-risk stale sections are the production-model comparison prose and the pricing table.
